items.q: When is deception in research ever acceptable?
items.correct: When demand characteristics would otherwise invalidate the study, with thorough debrief and ethics approval.
items.why: Students think BPS guidelines forbid deception outright. They actually permit it when justified, minimised, and followed by full debrief allowing withdrawal of data.

items.q: What is the difference between reliability and validity?
items.correct: Reliability is consistency; validity is whether it measures what it claims to.
items.misconception: They are essentially the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Reliability is for quantitative, validity for qualitative.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. A bathroom scale that adds 5kg every time is reliable (consistent) but not valid (wrong reading). You can be reliable without being valid.

items.q: What does a correlation of -0.8 indicate?
items.misconception: A weak relationship (because it is negative).
items.why: Students treat the negative sign as 'weak'. The sign shows DIRECTION (as one variable goes up, the other goes down); the absolute value shows STRENGTH. -0.8 is strong.

items.q: How long does information typically last in short-term memory without rehearsal, according to Peterson and Peterson?
items.why: Correct answer: 18 seconds. “30 seconds” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Whose 1932 'War of the Ghosts' study demonstrated reconstructive memory?
items.why: Correct answer: Bartlett. “Atkinson” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Atkinson and Shiffrin propose the multi-store model?
items.why: Exact answer: 1968 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
